macro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's Defense of Rabbi Ishmael and the Relevance of the Debate on Less Than Four Amot

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva insists Rabbi Ishmael never made the claim about less than four amot
The relevance of ruling that less than four amot makes a difference: implications for chatzer

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi's Principle: Identifying the Anonymous Student as Rabbi Meir

micro_segments.title: The principle that any student reporting Rabbi Ishmael's teaching to Rabbi Akiva must be Rabbi Meir
Rabbi Meir studied both from Rabbi Ishmael and Rabbi Akiva

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir's Story as a Scribe: The Kankantom Additive and the Sotah

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir's practice of adding kankantom to ink to prevent erasure
Rabbi Ishmael's objection: the law requires that writing be erasable for the Sotah
Rabbi Meir's response: his extreme care to prevent any accidental erasure
Contradiction between two versions of the story: who did Rabbi Meir study from first?
Resolution: Rabbi Meir studied from Rabbi Akiva first, then Rabbi Ishmael for the tradition, then returned to Rabbi Akiva

macro_segments.title: The Limits of Rabbi Meir's Brilliance: Understanding His Intellectual Acuity and Its Dangers

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Akha bar Chanina: Rabbi Meir was the most brilliant of his generation but his brilliance was so great that no one could fully understand him
Why we do not always rule like Rabbi Meir despite his brilliance: he could make false things appear true
Rabbi Meir's original name was Rabbi Nehorai; he was renamed for enlightening the eyes of the sages
Rabbi's account: seeing Rabbi Meir from behind was beneficial; seeing from in front would have been even better
Rabbi Meir's student Sumchus: forty-eight proofs for purity and forty-eight for impurity
An anonymous student who could give a hundred fifty ways to prove a sheretz was pure
Ravina's failed attempt to prove a sheretz is pure; Gemara's dismissal of such arguments

macro_segments.title: The Debate of Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel: Why We Rule Like Beit Hillel Despite Beit Shammai's Greater Brilliance

micro_segments.title: Three years they debated: who should the halacha follow? A heavenly voice declared both are words of the living God but we rule like Beit Hillel
Beit Hillel merited the halacha because they were pleasant, modest, taught both positions, and mentioned Beit Shammai's view first
Lesson: whoever humbles themselves, God elevates; whoever pursues greatness, greatness flees; whoever yields to the moment, the moment stands for him
Interpretation: Beit Hillel's engagement with all opinions made them the legitimate ruling position even if Beit Shammai was smarter

macro_segments.title: Better Not to Have Been Created: The Final Debate of Beit Shammai and Beit Hillel

micro_segments.title: Two and a half years they debated whether it would be better for humans not to have been created
Beit Shammai: better not to have been created; Beit Hillel: better to have been created; they voted and concluded it is better not to have been created
Now that we are created, we must investigate or feel our deeds before or after acting

topical_tags.term: Elu ve-elu divrei Elokim chayim (these and these are words of the living God)
